content:
# John Chapter 21 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

---

## 21:1 — "revealed/manifested" for Greek ἐφανέρωσεν and "Sea of Tiberias/Sea of Galilee"
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "manifested himself... at the sea of the Tiberias" | Preserves the Greek φανερόω as "manifest," retains the Johannine name "Tiberias" with article |
| LOGOS | "revealed himself... by the Sea of Tiberias" | Uses "revealed" as a more natural English equivalent; retains "Tiberias" |
| PNEUMA | "appeared... beside the Sea of Galilee" | Uses the common, accessible "appeared"; substitutes "Sea of Galilee" — the more widely recognized name |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "revealed himself... by the Sea of Tiberias." "Revealed" captures the theological weight of φανερόω better than "appeared," while "Tiberias" preserves the Johannine specificity. PNEUMA's "Sea of Galilee" is the synoptic name; John consistently uses "Tiberias" (cf. 6:1), and this should be honored.
**Status:** RESOLVED — consensus text follows LOGOS

---

## 21:3 — "I am going fishing" vs. "I go to fish" vs. "I'm going fishing"
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I go to fish" | Mirrors the Greek present tense ὑπάγω ἁλιεύειν literally |
| LOGOS | "I am going fishing" | Natural English rendering of the same Greek |
| PNEUMA | "I'm going fishing" | Contracted, conversational tone |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "I am going fishing." Minor stylistic variation only. EMET's rendering is awkward in English; PNEUMA's contraction is too informal for the translation register.
**Status:** RESOLVED — consensus text follows LOGOS

---

## 21:4 — "Just as day was breaking" vs. "early morning already having come" vs. "At dawn"
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And early [morning] already having come" | Preserves the genitive absolute construction πρωΐας δὲ ἤδη γενομένης |
| LOGOS | "Just as day was breaking" | Literary English, evocative phrasing |
| PNEUMA | "At dawn" | Concise, clear |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Just as day was breaking." It preserves the sense of transition and arrival that the Greek genitive absolute conveys, while being more readable than EMET's gloss and more evocative than PNEUMA's brevity.
**Status:** RESOLVED — consensus text follows LOGOS

---

## 21:5 — "Children" vs. "Fellows" for Greek Παιδία and "fish" vs. "food" for προσφάγιον
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Children, you do not have any food, do you?" | Literal παιδία = "children"; προσφάγιον = "food" (literally, something eaten with bread — not strictly "fish") |
| LOGOS | "Children, do you have any fish?" | Retains "Children" but renders προσφάγιον as "fish" since the context makes clear what food is meant |
| PNEUMA | "Fellows, have you caught any fish?" | Replaces "Children" with the informal "Fellows" to avoid implying they are literal children |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Children, do you have any fish?" "Children" (παιδία) is the correct address — it conveys Jesus' pastoral tone and authority, not biological age. PNEUMA's "Fellows" loses this. For προσφάγιον, EMET rightly notes it means "food" generically (a relish eaten with bread), but in this fishing context "fish" is the understood referent and avoids confusion. LOGOS captures both dimensions well.
**Status:** RESOLVED — consensus text follows LOGOS

---

## 21:6 — "quantity" vs. "multitude" vs. "so many fish"
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "because of the multitude of the fish" | Literal rendering of τοῦ πλήθους τῶν ἰχθύων |
| LOGOS | "because of the quantity of fish" | Formal equivalent with natural English |
| PNEUMA | "because there were so many fish in it" | Dynamic, explanatory |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "because of the quantity of fish." "Multitude" applied to fish sounds archaic; PNEUMA's paraphrase loses the compact phrasing of the Greek.
**Status:** RESOLVED — consensus text follows LOGOS

---

## 21:7 — "tied the outer garment around himself" vs. "put on his outer garment" vs. "put on his tunic" and "for he was stripped" vs. "stripped for work"
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "tied the outer garment around [himself] — for he was stripped" | Literal: διεζώσατο means "girded/tied around"; γυμνός = "naked/stripped" |
| LOGOS | "put on his outer garment, for he was stripped for work" | Adds "for work" to clarify that γυμνός means working in undergarments, not full nudity |
| PNEUMA | "put on his tunic (for he had stripped for work), jumped into the water" | Uses "tunic" instead of "outer garment"; adds "jumped into the water" for vividness |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "put on his outer garment, for he was stripped for work." EMET's "tied around" is more literal for διεζώσατο but less clear in English. PNEUMA's "tunic" is inaccurate — the ἐπενδύτης is the outer garment, not the inner tunic (χιτών). The clarification "for work" rightly prevents readers from imagining complete nudity.
**Status:** RESOLVED — consensus text follows LOGOS

---

## 21:8 — "small boat" vs. "boat" and "two hundred cubits" vs. "about a hundred yards"
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in the small boat... about two hundred cubits" | Literal: τῷ πλοιαρίῳ (diminutive) = "small boat"; δὲ ὡς ἀπὸ πηχῶν διακοσίων = "about two hundred cubits" |
| LOGOS | "in the boat... about a hundred yards off" | Drops diminutive; converts cubits to approximate modern measurement |
| PNEUMA | "with the boat... only about a hundred yards from shore" | Adds "only" for dramatic effect; modern measurement |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "in the boat... about a hundred yards off." EMET's "small boat" preserves the diminutive πλοιαρίῳ, which is a meaningful textual detail. However, using "cubits" is unhelpful for modern readers. LOGOS's conversion (200 cubits = roughly 100 yards) is accurate and accessible. PNEUMA's "only" is an interpretive addition.
**Status:** RESOLVED — consensus text follows LOGOS. Note: EMET's preservation of the diminutive "small boat" is acknowledged as textually faithful.

---

## 21:9 — "charcoal fire in place, with fish laid out on it" vs. "charcoal fire set, and fish lying on it" vs. "fish cooking over a charcoal fire"
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a charcoal fire set, and fish lying on [it], and bread" | Close to Greek word order; preserves κείμενα ("lying") |
| LOGOS | "a charcoal fire in place, with fish laid out on it, and bread" | "In place" for κειμένην; "laid out" for the fish |
| PNEUMA | "breakfast waiting for them — fish cooking over a charcoal fire, and some bread" | Adds "breakfast waiting"; interprets "lying on it" as "cooking over" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "a charcoal fire in place, with fish laid out on it, and bread." PNEUMA's "breakfast waiting for them" and "cooking" are interpretive expansions not in the Greek. The text says ὀψάριον ἐπικείμενον — fish placed/lying upon it — without specifying the cooking state. LOGOS's rendering is both accurate and clear.
**Status:** RESOLVED — consensus text follows LOGOS

---

## 21:12 — "Come and have breakfast" vs. "Come, eat breakfast" vs. "Now come and have some breakfast!"
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Come, eat breakfast" | Literal δεῦτε ἀριστήσατε |
| LOGOS | "Come and have breakfast" | Slightly more natural English |
| PNEUMA | "Now come and have some breakfast!" | Adds "Now" and exclamation for warmth |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Come and have breakfast." PNEUMA's additions ("Now," "some," exclamation mark) are editorial. EMET's "eat breakfast" is slightly stiff. LOGOS captures the hospitable tone without embellishment.
**Status:** RESOLVED — consensus text follows LOGOS

---

## 21:13 — "gave it to them, and so with the fish" vs. "gives it to them, and the fish likewise" vs. "served them the bread and the fish"
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "takes the bread and gives [it] to them, and the fish likewise" | Historical present tense preserved; "likewise" for ὁμοίως |
| LOGOS | "took the bread and gave it to them, and so with the fish" | Past tense; "and so with" for natural English |
| PNEUMA | "served them the bread and the fish" | Condensed; "served" interprets the giving action |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "served" imports a different connotation — the Greek λαμβάνει... δίδωσιν ("takes... gives") echoes eucharistic language (cf. 6:11), which "served" obscures. EMET's historical present is faithful but jarring in extended English narrative.
**Status:** RESOLVED — consensus text follows LOGOS

---

## 21:14 — "revealed" vs. "manifested" vs. "appeared"
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Yeshua was manifested" | Literal φανερόω; retains "Yeshua" |
| LOGOS | "Jesus was revealed" | φανερόω as "revealed" |
| PNEUMA | "Jesus had appeared" | Dynamic; past perfect tense |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Jesus was revealed." Consistent with v.1 resolution. φανερόω carries more theological weight than simple "appearing" — it implies a deliberate disclosure of identity.
**Status:** RESOLVED — consensus text follows LOGOS

---

## 21:15 — "son of John" vs. "son of Ioannes" for Ἰωάννου
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Simon [son] of Ioannes" | Transliterated Greek patronymic |
| LOGOS | "Simon, son of John" | Anglicized |
| PNEUMA | "Simon son of John" | Anglicized, no comma |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Simon, son of John." Anglicization is appropriate for clarity. EMET's transliterations (Ioannes, Petros, Yeshua) are consistently noted but not adopted in the consensus text, per standing policy.
**Status:** RESOLVED — consensus text follows LOGOS

---

## 21:15-17 — "Feed my lambs / Tend my sheep / Feed my sheep" vs. "Feed the lambs of me / Shepherd the sheep of me / Feed the sheep of me"
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Feed the lambs of me" / "Shepherd the sheep of me" / "Feed the sheep of me" | Preserves Greek word order and possessive; distinguishes βόσκε (feed) from ποίμαινε (shepherd) |
| LOGOS | "Feed my lambs" / "Tend my sheep" / "Feed my sheep" | Natural English possessive; "Tend" for ποίμαινε |
| PNEUMA | "Then feed my lambs" / "Then take care of my sheep" / "Then feed my sheep" | Adds "Then" as connector; "take care of" for ποίμαινε |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The critical divergence is the rendering of ποίμαινε in v.16: EMET's "Shepherd" is the most precise (ποιμαίνω = to shepherd/tend as a shepherd), LOGOS's "Tend" is adequate, PNEUMA's "take care of" is too generic. EMET's Greek word order ("the lambs of me") is not natural English. LOGOS's "Tend" is adopted over "Shepherd" for readability, though "Shepherd" is noted as the more precise rendering.
**Status:** RESOLVED — consensus text follows LOGOS. Marginal note: ποίμαινε could also be rendered "Shepherd."

---

## 21:18 — "Truly, truly" vs. "Amen, amen" vs. "I tell you the solemn truth" for Ἀμὴν ἀμὴν
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Amen, amen, I say to you" | Direct transliteration of the Hebrew/Aramaic formula |
| LOGOS | "Truly, truly, I say to you" | Standard formal English equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "I tell you the solemn truth" | Dynamic rendering that conveys the gravity without the doubled formula |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Truly, truly, I say to you." The doubled ἀμήν is a distinctive Johannine feature (appears 25 times in John, nowhere else in this doubled form). EMET's transliteration is defensible but less accessible. PNEUMA's paraphrase loses the distinctive doubled structure entirely. LOGOS preserves both the form and the meaning.
**Status:** RESOLVED — consensus text follows LOGOS

---

## 21:18 — "dress yourself / girded yourself" vs. "you were able to do as you liked" for ἐζώννυες σεαυτόν
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you girded yourself and walked where you wished" | Literal ζώννυμι = to gird/bind around |
| LOGOS | "you used to dress yourself and walk wherever you wanted" | "Dress" for modern comprehension of girding |
| PNEUMA | "you were able to do as you liked; you dressed yourself and went wherever you wanted to go" | Expands with interpretive "you were able to do as you liked" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "you used to dress yourself and walk wherever you wanted." EMET's "girded" is archaic for modern readers. PNEUMA's "you were able to do as you liked" is an interpretive expansion not in the Greek — it adds a gloss of autonomy that, while implied, goes beyond the text.
**Status:** RESOLVED — consensus text follows LOGOS

---

## 21:19 — Parenthetical framing
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And this he said signifying by what kind of death he will glorify the God." | No parentheses; literal σημαίνων = "signifying" |
| LOGOS | "(This he said to show by what kind of death he was to glorify God.)" | Parenthetical; "to show" for σημαίνων |
| PNEUMA | "Jesus said this to let him know by what kind of death he would glorify God." | No parentheses; "let him know" for σημαίνων |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's parenthetical framing. The clause is an editorial aside by the narrator, not dialogue, and parentheses appropriately signal this shift in voice. "To show" is preferable to PNEUMA's "to let him know" (which narrows the audience to Peter alone — the narrator is speaking to the reader).
**Status:** RESOLVED — consensus text follows LOGOS

---

## 21:20 — "leaned back against him" vs. "reclined... upon the breast of him" vs. "leaned over to Jesus"
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "who also reclined at the supper upon the breast of him" | Literal: ἀνέπεσεν ἐπὶ τὸ στῆθος αὐτοῦ |
| LOGOS | "the one who also had leaned back against him during the supper" | Natural English; preserves the physical gesture |
| PNEUMA | "the one who had leaned over to Jesus during supper" | "Leaned over" — changes direction of the gesture |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "leaned over to" misrepresents the posture — the Beloved Disciple leaned back against Jesus' chest (ἐπὶ τὸ στῆθος), not forward toward him. This matters for understanding ancient reclining dining posture. EMET's "upon the breast of him" is accurate but awkward.
**Status:** RESOLVED — consensus text follows LOGOS

---

## 21:21 — "Lord, what about this man?" vs. "Lord, and this one — what?" vs. "What about him, Lord?"
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Lord, and this [one] — what?" | Very literal: Κύριε, οὗτος δὲ τί; |
| LOGOS | "Lord, what about this man?" | Natural English question |
| PNEUMA | "What about him, Lord?" | Repositions "Lord" to end for conversational flow |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Lord, what about this man?" EMET's version preserves the abrupt, almost breathless syntax of the Greek (literally "Lord, but this one — what?"), which is stylistically interesting but unclear in English. PNEUMA's reordering loses the initial address to the Lord.
**Status:** RESOLVED — consensus text follows LOGOS

---

## 21:22 — "If it is my will that he remain" vs. "If I wish him to remain" vs. "If I want him to remain alive"
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "If I wish him to remain until I come" | Literal θέλω = "wish" |
| LOGOS | "If it is my will that he remain until I come" | More formal; "my will" for θέλω |
| PNEUMA | "If I want him to remain alive until I return" | Adds "alive" (interpretive); "return" for ἔρχομαι |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "If it is my will that he remain until I come." PNEUMA's "alive" is an interpretive addition — the Greek says only μένειν ("remain"), and the theological ambiguity is part of the text's meaning (remain alive? remain in ministry? remain faithful?). PNEUMA's "return" for ἔρχομαι is defensible but "come" preserves the parousia language.
**Status:** RESOLVED — consensus text follows LOGOS

---

## 21:23 — "the saying spread abroad among the brothers" vs. "this word went out to the brothers" vs. "the rumor spread among the community of believers"
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "this word went out to the brothers" | Literal: ἐξῆλθεν ὁ λόγος οὗτος εἰς τοὺς ἀδελφούς |
| LOGOS | "the saying spread abroad among the brothers" | "Spread abroad" conveys dissemination; "brothers" retained |
| PNEUMA | "the rumor spread among the community of believers" | "Rumor" has negative connotation; "community of believers" interprets ἀδελφούς |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the saying spread abroad among the brothers." PNEUMA's "rumor" imports a pejorative connotation not present in the Greek λόγος ("word/saying"). "Community of believers" is an over-interpretation of ἀδελφούς — while theologically valid, "brothers" is what the text says and should be preserved.
**Status:** RESOLVED — consensus text follows LOGOS

---

## 21:24 — "bearing witness" vs. "testifying" vs. "testifies" and "testimony is true" vs. "account... is accurate"
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the disciple testifying concerning these things... the testimony of him is true" | Literal μαρτυρέω/μαρτυρία; "true" for ἀληθής |
| LOGOS | "bearing witness about these things... his testimony is true" | "Bearing witness" — more formal register; "true" for ἀληθής |
| PNEUMA | "who testifies to these events... his account of these things is accurate" | "Account" for μαρτυρία; "accurate" for ἀληθής |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "bearing witness... his testimony is true." PNEUMA's "account" and "accurate" flatten the legal/courtroom connotations of μαρτυρία ("testimony/witness") and ἀληθής ("true" in the Johannine theological sense — not merely factually accurate but genuinely revelatory). This is the Gospel's closing authentication; "testimony" and "true" carry the full Johannine weight.
**Status:** RESOLVED — consensus text follows LOGOS

---

## 21:25 — "the world itself could not contain" vs. "not even the world itself would have room for" vs. "the whole world could not contain"
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "not even the world itself would have room for the books being written" | Literal: οὐδ᾽ αὐτὸν... τὸν κόσμον χωρῆσαι; preserves "not even" (οὐδέ) |
| LOGOS | "the world itself could not contain the books that would be written" | Smooth English; drops the emphatic "not even" |
| PNEUMA | "the whole world could not contain the books that would be written" | "Whole world" adds totality emphasis |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. EMET's "not even" (οὐδέ) is an accurate intensifier worth noting. PNEUMA's "whole world" is a reasonable dynamic equivalent of the emphatic construction but adds a word not strictly in the Greek.
**Status:** RESOLVED — consensus text follows LOGOS

---

## Summary Table

| Verse | Greek Term / Issue | EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic) | Resolution |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 21:1 | φανερόω / Sea name | "manifested" / "Tiberias" | "revealed" / "Tiberias" | "appeared" / "Galilee" | LOGOS — "revealed" / "Tiberias" |
| 21:5 | παιδία / προσφάγιον | "Children" / "food" | "Children" / "fish" | "Fellows" / "fish" | LOGOS — "Children" / "fish" |
| 21:7 | ἐπενδύτης / γυμνός | "outer garment" / "stripped" | "outer garment" / "stripped for work" | "tunic" / "stripped for work" | LOGOS — "outer garment, stripped for work" |
| 21:8 | πλοιαρίῳ / πηχῶν διακοσίων | "small boat" / "two hundred cubits" | "boat" / "hundred yards" | "boat" / "hundred yards" | LOGOS — modern measurement; EMET's diminutive noted |
| 21:9 | ἀνθρακιάν scene | "charcoal fire set, fish lying" | "charcoal fire in place, fish laid out" | "fish cooking over a charcoal fire" | LOGOS — no interpretive expansion |
| 21:13 | λαμβάνει / δίδωσιν | "takes... gives" (hist. present) | "took... gave" (past tense) | "served" | LOGOS — preserves eucharistic echo |
| 21:15-17 | ποίμαινε | "Shepherd" | "Tend" | "take care of" | LOGOS — "Tend"; "Shepherd" noted as more precise |
| 21:18 | ἀμὴν ἀμήν | "Amen, amen" | "Truly, truly" | "I tell you the solemn truth" | LOGOS — preserves doubled form |
| 21:18 | ἐζώννυες σεαυτόν | "girded yourself" | "dress yourself" | "you were able to do as you liked" | LOGOS — avoids archaism and expansion |
| 21:20 | ἀνέπεσεν ἐπὶ τὸ στῆθος | "reclined upon the breast" | "leaned back against him" | "leaned over to Jesus" | LOGOS — correct posture; PNEUMA's direction wrong |
| 21:22 | μένειν / ἔρχομαι | "remain" / "come" | "remain" / "come" | "remain alive" / "return" | LOGOS — no interpretive additions |
| 21:23 | λόγος / ἀδελφούς | "word" / "brothers" | "saying" / "brothers" | "rumor" / "community of believers" | LOGOS — neutral "saying"; preserves "brothers" |
| 21:24 | μαρτυρία / ἀληθής | "testimony" / "true" | "testimony" / "true" | "account" / "accurate" | LOGOS — full Johannine weight preserved |

**Overall pattern:** In this chapter, LOGOS and the consensus text align closely throughout. EMET provides valuable literal precision (diminutive forms, transliterations, historical present tense) that enriches the apparatus notes. PNEUMA's dynamic renderings occasionally overinterpret (21:5 "Fellows," 21:9 "cooking," 21:22 "alive," 21:23 "rumor") but offer pastoral insight in the commentary. The most significant divergence is PNEUMA's directional error in 21:20 ("leaned over" vs. "leaned back against"), which misrepresents the ancient dining posture.

---
*— SOPHIA, AI Arbiter, 2026-03-05*
